core_concept.principle: Atoms are composed of three fundamental subatomic particles: protons, neutrons, and electrons, each with distinct properties that determine atomic behavior.
core_concept.explanation: Protons and neutrons reside in the nucleus and account for nearly all atomic mass, while electrons orbit the nucleus and determine chemical properties. Understanding these particles is essential for explaining atomic structure, isotopes, and chemical bonding.

image.alt: Diagram showing protons and neutrons in nucleus with electrons orbiting
image.caption: Atoms consist of a dense nucleus surrounded by orbiting electrons.

sections.introduction: After Dalton proposed atoms were indivisible, experiments in the late 1800s and early 1900s revealed that atoms contain even smaller particles.

introduction_image.caption: Three key particles were discovered over a 35-year period.

key_concept.definition: Subatomic particles are the constituent particles of atoms: protons (positively charged), neutrons (no charge), and electrons (negatively charged).
key_concept.context: The discovery of these particles proved atoms are divisible and led to modern atomic theory. Each particle has specific mass, charge, and location within the atom.

image.alt: Table comparing mass and charge of protons, neutrons, and electrons
image.caption: Each subatomic particle has unique properties.

sections.introduction: Each subatomic particle has distinct properties that determine its role in atomic structure and chemical behavior.

classifications.value: Positively charged particles located in the nucleus
characteristics: Location: Tightly bound in the atomic nucleus
Number determines the element identity (atomic number)
classifications.behavior: Protons provide positive charge to balance electrons and contribute to atomic mass. The number of protons defines which element an atom is.

examples.explanation: Hydrogen has 1 proton; this single proton defines it as hydrogen. Adding a proton would make it helium.

image.alt: Hydrogen atom showing single proton in nucleus
image.caption: One proton = hydrogen element.

examples.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons; any atom with 6 protons is carbon, regardless of neutrons or electrons.

image.alt: Carbon atom showing 6 protons in nucleus
image.caption: Six protons = carbon element.

classifications.value: Neutral particles (no charge) located in the nucleus
characteristics: Location: Bound with protons in the atomic nucleus
Number can vary, creating isotopes of the same element
classifications.behavior: Neutrons add mass to the atom and stabilize the nucleus by reducing proton-proton repulsion. Different neutron numbers create isotopes.

examples.explanation: Both have 6 protons (carbon), but Carbon-12 has 6 neutrons while Carbon-14 has 8 neutrons. Both are carbon but different isotopes.

image.alt: Comparison of Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 nuclei
image.caption: Same protons, different neutrons = isotopes.

examples.explanation: Neutrons act as a buffer between positively charged protons, preventing them from repelling each other apart.

image.alt: Neutrons separating protons in nucleus
image.caption: Neutrons stabilize the nucleus.

classifications.value: Negatively charged particles orbiting the nucleus
characteristics: Location: Move in regions around the nucleus (electron cloud)
Number determines chemical properties and bonding behavior
classifications.behavior: Electrons are involved in chemical bonding and reactions. They are much lighter than protons and neutrons and occupy most of the atom's volume.

examples.explanation: When atoms bond, they share or transfer electrons, not protons or neutrons. Electron arrangement determines reactivity.

image.alt: Two atoms sharing electrons in a bond
image.caption: Electrons participate in chemical bonding.

examples.explanation: Losing electrons creates positive ions; gaining electrons creates negative ions. Proton number stays the same.

image.alt: Atom losing electron to become positive ion
image.caption: Electron gain or loss creates ions.

sections.introduction: Understanding the relative masses and charges of subatomic particles helps explain atomic structure and behavior.

process_flow.description: Protons and neutrons have nearly identical mass; electrons are about 1/1836 the mass of a proton.
process_flow.mechanism: This mass difference means nearly all atomic mass comes from the nucleus, while electrons contribute negligible mass.
process_flow.result: Atomic mass ≈ number of protons + number of neutrons.

image.alt: Scale showing proton/neutron mass vs electron mass
image.caption: Electrons are much lighter than protons and neutrons.

process_flow.description: Protons have +1 charge, electrons have -1 charge, neutrons have 0 charge.
process_flow.mechanism: Equal numbers of protons and electrons make atoms electrically neutral; imbalance creates ions.
process_flow.result: Net charge = (number of protons) - (number of electrons).

image.alt: Charges of protons, neutrons, and electrons
image.caption: Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.

process_flow.description: Protons and neutrons are in the tiny, dense nucleus; electrons occupy the large space around the nucleus.
process_flow.mechanism: The nucleus contains 99.9% of atomic mass but occupies only a tiny fraction of atomic volume.
process_flow.result: Atoms are mostly empty space with a dense central nucleus.

image.alt: Diagram showing nucleus and electron cloud regions
image.caption: Nucleus is tiny but massive; electrons occupy most volume.

process_flow.description: Protons determine element identity; electrons determine chemical behavior; neutrons affect nuclear stability.
process_flow.mechanism: Chemical reactions involve electron rearrangement; nuclear reactions involve changes to protons and neutrons.
process_flow.result: Chemistry is the study of electron behavior; nuclear physics studies the nucleus.

image.alt: Electrons involved in chemical reaction diagram
image.caption: Electrons drive chemical reactions and bonding.

sections.introduction: The number of protons in an atom (atomic number) determines the element, while the arrangement of particles determines atomic properties.

key_concept.definition: Atomic number (Z) = number of protons in the nucleus. This number uniquely identifies each element on the periodic table.
key_concept.context: In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons. The mass number (A) = protons + neutrons.

image.alt: Periodic table showing atomic numbers for elements
image.caption: Atomic number increases by 1 for each element across the periodic table.

principles.statement: In a neutral atom, the number of protons equals the number of electrons, resulting in zero net charge.
principles.application: To find electron count in a neutral atom, use the atomic number. For example, oxygen (Z=8) has 8 protons and 8 electrons when neutral.
principles.analogy: Like a balanced bank account: deposits (protons) equal withdrawals (electrons), so the balance (charge) is zero.
principles.example: Sodium atom: 11 protons, 11 electrons, net charge = 0.

image.alt: Diagram showing equal protons and electrons in neutral atom
image.caption: Equal protons and electrons = neutral atom.

principles.statement: When atoms gain or lose electrons, they become charged particles called ions.
principles.application: Positive ions (cations) have fewer electrons than protons; negative ions (anions) have more electrons than protons.
principles.analogy: Like an unbalanced bank account: more deposits than withdrawals = positive balance; more withdrawals = negative balance.
principles.example: Sodium ion (Na⁺): 11 protons, 10 electrons, net charge = +1.

image.alt: Comparison of neutral atom and ion
image.caption: Losing or gaining electrons creates charged ions.

sections.introduction: Given atomic number, mass number, and charge, you can calculate the number of protons, neutrons, and electrons in any atom or ion.

worked_examples.title: Finding Particle Counts in a Neutral Atom
worked_examples.problem: An atom of carbon-12 has atomic number 6 and mass number 12. How many protons, neutrons, and electrons does it contain?

step_by_step.explanation: Atomic number equals the number of protons. This defines the element.
Mass number equals protons plus neutrons. Subtract protons to find neutrons.
In a neutral atom, electrons equal protons to balance charge.
Verify the counts make sense for carbon-12.

worked_examples.final_answer: Carbon-12 contains 6 protons, 6 neutrons, and 6 electrons.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Atomic number = protons; Mass number = protons + neutrons; Neutral atom has equal protons and electrons.

image.alt: Carbon-12 atom showing 6 protons, 6 neutrons, 6 electrons
image.caption: Carbon-12 has equal numbers of protons and neutrons.

worked_examples.title: Finding Particle Counts in an Ion
worked_examples.problem: An oxide ion (O²⁻) has atomic number 8 and mass number 16. How many protons, neutrons, and electrons does it contain?

step_by_step.explanation: Atomic number equals the number of protons. This does not change for ions.
Mass number equals protons plus neutrons. Neutrons do not change in ion formation.
The 2- charge means the ion has 2 more electrons than protons.
Verify the charge: 8 positive charges from protons, 10 negative charges from electrons = net -2 charge.

worked_examples.final_answer: The oxide ion (O²⁻) contains 8 protons, 8 neutrons, and 10 electrons.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Ions form when electrons are gained or lost; protons and neutrons remain unchanged. Charge = protons - electrons.

image.alt: Oxide ion showing 8 protons, 8 neutrons, 10 electrons
image.caption: Gaining 2 electrons creates the 2- charge on oxide ion.

sections.introduction: Although electrons occupy most of an atom's volume, nearly all the mass is concentrated in the tiny nucleus.

categories.description: The nucleus contains over 99.9% of an atom's total mass despite being extremely small.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Protons and neutrons each have mass of approximately 1 atomic mass unit. Electrons have mass of about 1/1836 amu, making their contribution negligible.

examples.explanation: Carbon-12 has 6 protons (6 amu) + 6 neutrons (6 amu) + 6 electrons (0.0033 amu) = 12.0033 amu total. Electrons contribute only 0.03% of mass.

image.alt: Pie chart showing mass distribution in atom
image.caption: Nucleus contains virtually all atomic mass.

categories.description: Electrons occupy nearly all of an atom's volume despite contributing negligible mass.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The electron cloud extends about 10,000 times farther from the nucleus than the nucleus radius. This creates mostly empty space.

examples.explanation: If an atom were the size of a football stadium, the nucleus would be a marble at center field, and electrons would be like gnats buzzing in the stands.

image.alt: Stadium analogy for atomic size
image.caption: Atoms are mostly empty space occupied by electrons.

sections.introduction: Each subatomic particle was discovered through careful experimentation that revealed properties not explained by previous atomic models.

process_types.function: Cathode ray tube experiments showed atoms contain negative particles

primary_example.explanation: Thomson showed cathode rays bent toward positive plates, proving they carried negative charge. The consistent charge-to-mass ratio regardless of gas type proved electrons are universal components of all atoms.

image.alt: Cathode ray tube with deflection plates
image.caption: Thomson's experiment proved electrons exist in all atoms.

examples.explanation: Thomson calculated e/m ratio, showing electrons are much lighter than hydrogen atoms.

process_types.function: Bombarding nitrogen with alpha particles produced hydrogen nuclei
goals: Showed protons are fundamental to all nuclei

primary_example.explanation: Rutherford fired alpha particles at nitrogen gas and detected hydrogen nuclei (protons) among the products, proving protons are components of other atomic nuclei, not just hydrogen.

image.alt: Rutherford's proton discovery experiment
image.caption: Rutherford identified protons as nuclear components.

examples.explanation: Showed that hydrogen nuclei are the positive particles in all atomic nuclei.

process_types.function: Bombarding beryllium with alpha particles produced neutral radiation

primary_example.explanation: Chadwick showed that radiation from beryllium could knock protons out of paraffin wax but was not deflected by electric or magnetic fields, proving it consisted of neutral particles with mass similar to protons.

image.alt: Chadwick's neutron discovery experiment
image.caption: Chadwick proved neutrons exist as neutral nuclear particles.

examples.explanation: Measured neutron mass as slightly greater than proton mass, explaining isotopic mass differences.

sections.relationship: The discovery of all three subatomic particles completed the basic model of atomic structure and enabled modern chemistry and nuclear physics.

summary: Atoms consist of three fundamental subatomic particles: protons (positive charge, in nucleus), neutrons (no charge, in nucleus), and electrons (negative charge, orbiting nucleus). Protons determine element identity through atomic number; neutrons contribute to mass and nuclear stability; electrons determine chemical behavior. In neutral atoms, protons equal electrons; ions form when this balance changes. Nearly all atomic mass resides in the tiny nucleus, while electrons occupy most atomic volume. Protons and neutrons have similar mass (~1 amu each); electrons are ~1/1836 the mass of a proton. Atomic number = proton count; mass number = protons + neutrons; net charge = protons - electrons. Understanding these particles explains atomic structure, isotopes, ions, and chemical bonding. Each particle was discovered through landmark experiments: Thomson (electron, 1897), Rutherford (proton, 1917-1919), and Chadwick (neutron, 1932). This three-particle model forms the foundation of modern chemistry and physics.
